events.title: Paris Agreement Adopted at COP21
events.detail: 195 nations adopted the Paris Agreement on December 12, 2015 in Le Bourget, France, committing to limit global average temperature rise to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels — with efforts to limit warming to 1.5°C. The accord required all countries to submit nationally determined contributions (NDCs) and update them every five years with increasing ambition.

events.title: Paris Agreement Enters Into Force
events.detail: The Paris Agreement formally entered into force on November 4, 2016 — less than a year after adoption — after ratification by 55 countries accounting for more than 55% of global greenhouse gas emissions. This set a record as the fastest multilateral environmental agreement to achieve legal force, with 196 parties eventually joining.

events.title: Solar Auction Prices Hit Record Lows
events.detail: Auction prices for utility-scale solar photovoltaic electricity fell to record lows in 2016, with bids below $30/MWh in Chile ($29.1/MWh) and the UAE ($24.2/MWh). Global average solar PV module prices fell to under $0.40/W — an 80% reduction since 2009 — confirming solar's rapid cost trajectory toward cost-competitiveness with fossil fuels.

events.title: Morocco's Noor I Concentrated Solar Plant Opens
events.detail: Morocco inaugurated the Noor I concentrated solar power (CSP) plant near Ouarzazate in February 2016, the first phase of what became Africa's largest solar complex. Noor I has 160 MW capacity and storage that allows electricity generation for up to 3 hours after sunset. Morocco was targeting 52% renewable electricity by 2030, making it a model for the developing world.

events.title: Trump Announces US Withdrawal from Paris
events.detail: President Donald Trump announced on June 1, 2017, that the United States would withdraw from the Paris Agreement, calling it unfair to American workers and businesses. The announcement was a major blow to global climate diplomacy, but triggered a 'We Are Still In' counter-movement from US states, cities, and businesses pledging to honor Paris commitments. The withdrawal became effective November 4, 2020, the day after Election Day.

events.title: China Becomes World's Largest Solar Installer
events.detail: China installed a record 53 GW of solar PV in 2017, more than the rest of the world combined, bringing its total capacity to 130 GW — the highest globally. China's massive deployment drove down global solar panel prices by over 30% that year alone, making the technology affordable for markets worldwide. China also led global wind and hydropower capacity.

events.title: IPCC Special Report: 1.5°C Warming Threshold
events.detail: The IPCC released its landmark Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5°C (SR1.5) in October 2018, showing that limiting warming to 1.5°C rather than 2°C would significantly reduce climate risks. The report found the world had already warmed ~1°C and had roughly 12 years (to 2030) to cut emissions by ~45% to stay on a 1.5°C pathway. It made net-zero CO2 emissions by ~2050 the central target for climate policy.

events.title: California Mandates 100% Clean Electricity by 2045
events.detail: California Governor Jerry Brown signed SB 100 into law on September 10, 2018, requiring 100% of the state's electricity to come from clean sources by 2045 — the most ambitious renewable energy mandate by any large US state. California, the world's fifth-largest economy, also pledged carbon neutrality by 2045, serving as a global model for climate legislation even as the federal government retreated from Paris.

events.title: IRENA: Solar & Wind Reach Cost Parity with Fossil Fuels
events.detail: The International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) reported in 2018 that solar PV and onshore wind were increasingly cost-competitive with, or cheaper than, new fossil fuel power plants across much of the world — without subsidies. The average cost of electricity from new solar plants had fallen 73% since 2010, and onshore wind by 23%. This was a watershed moment marking renewable energy's transition from subsidy-dependent to market-driven.

events.title: UK Becomes First Major Economy to Pass Net-Zero Law
events.detail: The United Kingdom became the first major economy to legislate a net-zero greenhouse gas emissions target when Parliament passed an amendment to the Climate Change Act on June 26, 2019, requiring the UK to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050. The law was backed by cross-party support and recommendations from the UK's independent Climate Change Committee, and set a legal precedent followed by dozens of nations.

events.title: EU Launches the European Green Deal
events.detail: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen launched the European Green Deal on December 11, 2019 — a comprehensive package of legislation aiming to make the EU climate-neutral by 2050. Key elements included a European Climate Law, a new 2030 target of at least 55% emissions reduction, the 'Fit for 55' package, Hydrogen Strategy, and the €1 trillion+ green investment plan. It became the world's most ambitious coordinated climate governance framework.

events.title: EU Renewables Surpass Coal Power for First Time
events.detail: In 2020, renewable energy sources (wind, solar, hydro, and biomass) generated more electricity in the European Union than coal and lignite combined for the first time in history. Wind alone accounted for 14% of EU electricity, while solar contributed 5%. The milestone came a decade after renewables began their rapid expansion, accelerated by falling costs and the EU's binding renewable energy targets.

events.title: UK Offshore Wind Breaks Global Auction Price Record
events.detail: The UK's third Contract for Difference (CfD) auction in September 2019 secured offshore wind at a record-low £39.65/MWh — well below the wholesale electricity price and cheaper than new gas. Projects included Hornsea 2 (1.4 GW) and Dogger Bank (3.6 GW). This demonstrated offshore wind had definitively crossed the cost-competitiveness threshold, ending the need for above-market subsidies in mature markets.

events.title: COVID-19 Recovery Packages Channel $500B+ to Clean Energy
events.detail: Following the COVID-19 economic shock, governments committed over $500 billion in green recovery spending globally by late 2020, channeled to clean energy, EVs, building retrofits, and green hydrogen. The EU's €750 billion NextGenerationEU package required 37% of funds for climate goals. South Korea's Green New Deal and China's infrastructure stimulus also targeted clean energy, marking the largest single surge in climate-aligned public spending to date.

events.title: US Rejoins Paris Agreement Under Biden
events.detail: On his first day in office, January 20, 2021, President Biden signed an executive order beginning the process of rejoining the Paris Agreement. The US formally rejoined on February 19, 2021 — just 107 days after Trump's withdrawal took effect. Biden also pledged to cut US greenhouse gas emissions 50–52% below 2005 levels by 2030, effectively doubling the prior US commitment.

events.title: IEA: No New Fossil Fuel Projects Compatible with Net-Zero
events.detail: The International Energy Agency's landmark 'Net Zero by 2050' report, published May 2021, concluded that there must be no new oil, gas, or coal development beyond projects already approved if the world is to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050. The report — from the historically pro-fossil fuel agency — was a watershed moment in climate economics, calling for global clean energy investment to triple by 2030.

events.title: COP26 Glasgow: Coal Phase-Down & Methane Pledge
events.detail: COP26 in Glasgow (November 2021) achieved the first explicit commitment to 'phase down' coal in the Glasgow Climate Pact, agreed by nearly 200 countries. Over 100 nations signed the Global Methane Pledge to cut methane emissions 30% by 2030. The summit also secured over $100 billion annually in climate finance commitments (delayed from 2020 target) and completed the Paris Agreement's rulebook. Critics noted NDCs still projected ~2.4°C warming.

events.title: Global EV Sales Hit 6.75 Million — Doubling in One Year
events.detail: Global electric vehicle sales reached 6.75 million in 2021 — nearly double the 3.2 million sold in 2020. EVs captured about 9% of global car sales, up from 4% a year earlier. China led with 3.3 million units, followed by Europe (2.3 million) and the US (630,000). Battery costs had fallen to ~$130/kWh by year-end, down 89% from 2010 levels.

events.title: US Inflation Reduction Act: $369B for Climate
events.detail: President Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) into law on August 16, 2022 — the largest climate investment in US history at $369 billion in clean energy and climate provisions over 10 years. The IRA used tax credits and incentives to drive solar, wind, EVs, hydrogen, and advanced manufacturing. Analysts projected it would cut US emissions 40% by 2030 vs 2005 levels and catalyze $3+ trillion in private investment.

events.title: Global Solar Capacity Crosses 1 Terawatt Milestone
events.detail: In 2022, global installed solar photovoltaic capacity crossed 1 terawatt (1,000 GW) — a milestone that seemed impossible when the technology was in its infancy. It took 40 years to reach the first 100 GW (by 2012), but only another decade to add 900 GW more, reflecting exponential growth. Solar was adding roughly 250+ GW per year by 2022 and generating around 4% of global electricity.

events.title: COP27: Historic Loss and Damage Fund Established
events.detail: COP27 in Sharm el-Sheikh, Egypt (November 2022) achieved a historic breakthrough by establishing a dedicated 'loss and damage' fund to compensate vulnerable nations for climate impacts they did not cause. It was the first time rich countries formally agreed to financial responsibility for harm from climate change. The fund's architecture was debated through 2023, with pledges beginning at COP28.

events.title: China Installs 217 GW Solar in a Single Year — Shatters Record
events.detail: China installed a staggering 217 GW of solar PV capacity in 2023 — more than the entire world installed in 2021 — pushing its total solar capacity past 600 GW. China's solar investment exceeded $100 billion, driven by low panel costs, aggressive manufacturing subsidies, and provincial renewable targets. The country generated roughly 900 TWh from solar in 2023, overtaking Germany's total electricity consumption.

events.title: IEA: Clean Energy Investment Outpaces Fossil Fuels Globally
events.detail: The IEA's World Energy Investment 2023 report showed that clean energy investment surpassed $1.7 trillion — for the first time overtaking new fossil fuel supply investment by nearly 2:1. For every $1 spent on fossil fuels, $1.70 was being invested in clean energy. Solar alone attracted over $380 billion, exceeding oil investment for the first time. The IEA called 2023 a 'new era for energy investment.'

events.title: COP28 Dubai: Historic Call to Transition Away from Fossil Fuels
events.detail: COP28 in Dubai (November–December 2023) produced the UAE Consensus — the first climate agreement explicitly calling for a 'transition away from fossil fuels' in energy systems, to be achieved 'in a just, orderly and equitable manner.' The deal also included commitments to triple global renewable capacity and double energy efficiency by 2030, and operationalized the Loss and Damage Fund. It was the first formal global acknowledgment that oil, gas, and coal must be phased out.

events.title: Global EV Sales Exceed 14 Million — One in Five Cars Sold Globally
events.detail: Global electric vehicle sales hit approximately 14 million in 2023, reaching 18% of all new car sales worldwide — compared to just 4% in 2020. China dominated with 8.1 million EVs (36% of its car market), while Europe sold 3.2 million. Battery electric vehicles (BEVs) now have cumulative installed base of over 40 million globally, displacing significant oil demand.

events.title: EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism Enters Transitional Phase
events.detail: The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) entered its transitional phase on October 1, 2023, covering imports of steel, cement, aluminum, fertilizers, electricity, and hydrogen. CBAM requires importers to report embedded carbon emissions, and from 2026 will require payment for those emissions at EU carbon prices — effectively applying the EU Emissions Trading System to imports. It is the world's first carbon border tax and a major trade policy innovation.

events.title: India Surpasses 100 GW Installed Solar Capacity
events.detail: India surpassed 100 GW of installed solar capacity in 2024, reaching a milestone set as a 2022 target by PM Modi. India is now the world's third-largest solar market, after China and the EU. The country aims for 500 GW of non-fossil power by 2030 and 40% of its electricity from renewables. Major projects include the Bhadla Solar Park (2.7 GW, world's largest), Rewa (750 MW), and Pavagada (2.05 GW).

sources.name: Ministry of New & Renewable Energy India

events.title: Global Clean Energy Investment Exceeds $2 Trillion for First Time
events.detail: Global clean energy investment surpassed $2 trillion in 2024 for the first time ever, according to IEA and BloombergNEF data. Solar accounted for nearly half ($500B+), followed by EVs and batteries (~$350B), grid infrastructure (~$400B), and wind (~$250B). For the first time, clean energy investment in emerging markets exceeded that in developed economies. The milestone reflected that clean energy had become the world's primary energy investment destination.

events.title: World's Largest Direct Air Capture Plant Opens in Iceland
events.detail: Climeworks opened its 'Mammoth' direct air capture (DAC) plant in Iceland in May 2024, capable of removing 36,000 tonnes of CO₂ per year from the atmosphere — ten times the capacity of its predecessor 'Orca.' Powered by Iceland's geothermal energy, captured CO₂ is injected into basalt rock formations where it mineralizes permanently. The plant, though expensive ($400–1,000/tonne), demonstrated commercial-scale DAC is technically feasible.

events.title: Global Solar Capacity Crosses 2 Terawatt Milestone
events.detail: Global installed solar photovoltaic capacity reached approximately 2 terawatts (2,000 GW) in 2025, just three years after crossing the 1 TW threshold in 2022. Solar was adding over 500 GW per year, generating roughly 8–10% of global electricity. The exponential growth trajectory — doubling in capacity roughly every 3 years — cemented solar as the world's fastest-growing energy source and the cheapest form of electricity in history.

events.title: Amazon Deforestation Under Lula Drops 50% Below 2021 Peak
events.detail: Brazil's INPE data confirmed that Amazon deforestation in 2023 fell 50% compared to the peak year of 2021 under the Bolsonaro government, reaching the lowest levels since 2016. President Lula da Silva committed at COP28 to zero Amazon deforestation by 2030. Brazil also pledged $2 billion to the Amazon Fund and reactivated the Amazon Council, while enforcement actions against illegal deforestation increased dramatically.

events.title: 2024: Record Year — 585 GW of Renewables Added Globally
events.detail: In 2024, the world added approximately 585 GW of renewable energy capacity — a new annual record — bringing total global renewable capacity to around 4,448 GW. Solar additions of ~450 GW and wind additions of ~117 GW were both records. Renewables accounted for over 90% of all new power capacity added globally. The additions were enough to power roughly 400 million homes.

events.title: Global EV Sales Exceed 20 Million — One in Four New Cars Sold
events.detail: Global electric vehicle sales exceeded 20 million units in 2025, reaching approximately 25% of all new car sales worldwide — a landmark milestone confirmed by IEA analysis in April 2026. This compared with 17 million EVs (20%) in 2024 and just 3 million in 2020. China maintained its dominant position, and battery storage emerged as the fastest-growing power technology with 110 GW of new grid storage added. The EV surge is displacing an estimated 2 million barrels of oil per day in global demand and accelerating grid storage deployment.

events.title: 2025: Record 692 GW Added — Solar Overtakes All Energy Sources in Global Growth
events.detail: The world added a record 692 GW of renewable energy capacity in 2025, according to IRENA's Renewable Capacity Statistics released April 11, 2026, bringing the global total to 5,149 GW — nearly 50% of all global power capacity. Solar PV alone contributed 510 GW of new capacity and 600 TWh of new generation — the largest single-year increase ever recorded for any power technology, prompting the IEA to declare solar the #1 source of global energy supply growth for the first time in history. Wind added 159 GW. China reached 1,202 GW of installed solar capacity, while Africa recorded a 15.9% growth rate — its highest ever.

events.title: EU 2040 Climate Law Enters Into Force: World's Most Ambitious Legally Binding Target
events.detail: Regulation EU 2026/667 entered into force on April 7, 2026, legally committing the European Union to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions by 90% by 2040 compared to 1990 levels — the most ambitious legally binding medium-term climate target of any major economy. The law sets a clear trajectory from the 2030 target (−55%) to carbon neutrality by 2050, with up to 5% delivered via international carbon credits. Final European Parliament approval came February 9, 2026, with Council ratification on March 5. The law sends a decisive long-term investment signal to European industry and carbon-intensive sectors.
